name: Striated
classification_notes: The foundry describes Striated as a mixture of grotesque and geometric with formalised elements, placing it firmly in a geometric grotesque subcategory within the sans-serif category.
summary: A display-oriented geometric grotesque with three weights and matching italics, covering Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Designed with display use in mind, Striated blends grotesque and geometric influences with what the foundry describes as heavily formalised elements. Six styles across three weights serve both print and screen applications, and broad Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it suitable for multilingual work. OpenType features include stylistic sets and superiors, offering flexibility for varied typographic contexts.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe Striated as a display typeface categorised as a mixture between a grotesque and a geometric with a pinch of heavily formalised elements, available in three weights with matching italics for use in print and on screen.

name: No. 43
classification_notes: No. 43 is explicitly described as a reverse-contrast typeface in the tradition of 19th century merchandise typefaces, combining broad-nib and pointed-nib pen influences. Reverse-contrast display is the most accurate subcategory.
summary: A reverse-contrast display typeface drawing on 19th century merchandise letterforms, shaped by both broad-nib and pointed-nib pen traditions.
description: No. 43 takes its cues from 19th century merchandise typography, combining the influence of a steep-angled broad-nib pen with the character of a pointed nib. The result is a reverse-contrast display face suited to packaging, signage and titling work, where it benefits from generous surrounding space, particularly in its ornamented form. Available in four styles across Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe No. 43 as a reverse-contrast typeface in the tradition of 19th century merchandise typefaces, citing two major influences: writing with a broad-nib pen held at a very steep angle, and writing with a pointed-nib pen. They note it demands sufficient space around it, especially in its ornamented form, and recommend it for packaging, boards, signs and digital and analogue titling.

name: Traktor
classification_notes: The foundry describes Traktor as a mixed serif typeface with humanist, roman and gothic influences, placing it at the intersection of several serif traditions rather than firmly within one subcategory.
summary: A mixed serif with humanist, roman and gothic influences suited to labels, packaging and editorial settings, available as variable fonts with Cyrillic support.
description: Traktor draws on three distinct serif traditions, humanist, roman and gothic, blending them into a single typeface with eight styles across four weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold with matching italics. The family supports an extended Latin character set alongside a basic Cyrillic, making it serviceable across multiple European languages. Variable font files are included, and the design is positioned for label, packaging and editorial use as well as short passages of text.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe Traktor as a mixed serif typeface carrying humanist, roman and gothic influences, suitable for labels, packaging, editorial and book settings or short passages of text, and note that it includes a basic Cyrillic character set and variable font support.

name: No. 3 Series Narrow
classification_notes: Described explicitly as having humanist letter characteristics, with forms derived from Renaissance movable type and the rotational logic of italic broad-nib pen strokes, placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A narrow-width cut of a humanist serif family rooted in Renaissance pen-writing logic, tracing how early type designers adapted handwriting habits into movable type.
description: No. 3 Series Narrow is the condensed variant of a three-width family designed around the transition from broad-nib cursive writing to Renaissance movable type. The lowercase a, d, p and q are constructed from the theoretical rotational strokes of italic calligraphy, with the slope removed but the underlying structure preserved. Available in Regular, Bold and Heavy, it is suited to titling, visual identities, editorial work and screen applications.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe No. 3 Series as a typeface for large-scale applications including titling, identities, books, magazines and websites, covering three widths: Normal, Narrow and Poster. The design revisits how Renaissance type designers adapted existing writing habits into a reproducible type system, drawing on historical sources and specimen comparisons. The letterforms are particularly influenced by cursive writing, with the principle behind key lowercase letters based on rotating italic letters written with a broad-nib pen to an upright angle while retaining the underlying theoretical strokes.

name: No. 3 Series
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as having the characteristics of humanist letters and is rooted in the Renaissance transition from pen-based writing to movable type, with forms derived from the rotation of broad-nib italic strokes — firmly placing it in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A humanist serif family in three widths, developed by revisiting the Renaissance shift from pen-written letters to movable type, with lowercase forms derived from the rotation of broad-nib italic strokes.
description: The family spans four weights across three distinct widths — Normal, Narrow and Poster — making it suitable for titling, identities, books, magazines and screen use. Its lowercase a, d, p and q are built on the theoretical strokes of italic handwriting with the slope removed, preserving the pen's underlying geometry in upright forms. The result sits between calligraphic tradition and speculative type history, imagining how Renaissance punchcutters might have deviated from convention.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe No. 3 Series as a typeface for large-scale applications including titling, visual identities, books, magazines and websites. They explain that it carries the characteristics of humanist letters and covers three widths — Normal, Narrow and Poster — and that it is substantially influenced by writing and cursive writing. The design revisits the transition from pen to movable type during the Renaissance, with the lowercase a, d, p and q shaped by the theoretical strokes of italic handwriting written with a broad nib, with the slope reduced to zero but the underlying geometry retained.

name: No. 3 Series Poster
classification_notes: The typeface draws explicitly on Renaissance movable type principles and humanist letterforms, with strong calligraphic and cursive writing influences informing the construction of its lowercase characters.
summary: A wide display cut of No. 3 Series, built from a study of Renaissance type-cutting and the transition from broad-nib writing to movable type.
description: No. 3 Series Poster is the widest member of a three-width family, alongside Normal and Narrow, designed for titling, identities and large-scale applications. Its lowercase construction follows the principle of rotating italic letters written with a broad nib pen, reducing the slope to vertical while preserving the underlying stroke logic. The result sits between historical revival and speculative interpretation, imagining alternative paths from Renaissance handwriting into type.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe No. 3 Series as a typeface for large-scale use including titling, identities, books, magazines and websites, with humanist characteristics across three widths. The design revisits the transition from pen-written letterforms to movable type during the Renaissance, drawing on historical sources and specimen comparisons. The construction of letters like a, d, p and q is based on the theoretical rotation of italic broad-nib strokes, with the slope removed but the underlying form preserved.

name: Rhizome
classification_notes: Rhizome is built on a geometric construction juxtaposed with classical proportions and wedge-shaped details, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition while drawing on early 20th-century typographic ideals.
summary: A geometric sans with classical proportions and wedge-shaped details, designed for both text setting and display use.
description: Released in 2017, Rhizome draws on early 20th-century typographic ideals, pairing geometric construction with letterform proportions borrowed from serif traditions. The family runs across six weights from UltraLight to Heavy, each with a matching oblique, giving it range across editorial and identity contexts. Wedge-shaped details add a quiet distinctiveness without departing from functional legibility.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe Rhizome as an allround typeface suited to large applications and text setting alike, combining a glimpse into the past with what was considered visionary in typography at the start of the 20th century. A geometric construction is set against classical proportions, with letterforms drawn from serif traditions and details shaped by a wedge.

name: Plateau Five
classification_notes: The foundry describes Plateau Five as a display typeface rooted in transitional period forms between Baroque and Neo-Classicism, with prominent serifs and a rigid underlying structure, placing it firmly in the display serif category with transitional characteristics.
summary: A display serif drawing on transitional-era Baroque and Neo-Classical letterforms, built around a principle of optically equalised character widths.
description: Plateau Five combines the prominent serifs and structural rigidity of the transitional period between Baroque and Neo-Classicism with an unusual design principle: most Latin character widths are optically equal, an idea the foundry roots in the social philosophies of 1920s modernity. Italics are present across all four weights but their expressive quality is deliberately restrained relative to convention. The family covers Latin Extended character sets with standard ligatures and a broad set of accented characters.
foundry_blurb: Road to Venice Type describe Plateau Five as a display typeface whose forms are largely drawn from the transitional period between Baroque and Neo-Classicism, with an underlying rigid structure and prominent serifs. Its defining principle is even distribution, inspired by the social ideas of modernity that emerged in the 1920s, resulting in most Latin character widths being optically equal. The italics, while present, are intentionally muted in their expressiveness.
